groups.title: Start
groups.summary: Install, project shape, routing and target choices.

groups.title: Tac frontend
groups.summary: Templates, companions, workers and browser-local data.

groups.title: Yon backend
groups.summary: Routes, middleware, validation, FYLO and observability.

groups.title: Cookbook
groups.summary: Production-shaped recipes that combine Tac, Yon and FYLO.

introduction.title: Introduction
introduction.summary: Tachyon is a polyglot, file-system-routed full-stack framework distributed through the standalone ty binary. Tac is the frontend layer, Yon is the backend/runtime layer, and FYLO is the document store beneath both.

sections.heading: Install
sections.body: Tachyon ships as the standalone `ty` binary — no package manager or runtime toolchain required. The installer also pulls the `fylo` (document store) and `chex` (schema validation) binaries it drives at runtime. Scaffold an app and start the shape-aware dev server: Tachyon detects whether your app has a client/, a server/, or both, and serves the right thing on one port.
sections.code:
curl -fsSL https://tachyon.del.ma/install.sh | sh
ty init my-app --name "My App"
cd my-app
ty serve

sections.heading: Layout
sections.body: Pages live in client/pages, components in client/components, workers in client/workers, and backend routes in server/routes. Shared browser assets and data live under client/shared.
sections.code:
client/
  pages/tac.html
  components/hero/tac.html
  workers/language/rust/tac.rs
  shared/assets/  shared/data/
server/
  routes/posts/yon.js

routing.title: Routing
routing.summary: Both layers route from the file system: a directory is a path, and dynamic segments use _slug directories that become :slug parameters at runtime.

sections.heading: Frontend pages
sections.body: client/pages/tac.html serves /, client/pages/docs/tac.html serves /docs, and client/pages/docs/_topic/tac.html serves /docs/:topic — the page you are reading uses exactly that dynamic segment. An ancestor page containing a slot element becomes a reusable shell for its descendants.
sections.code:
client/pages/
  tac.html          -> /
  docs/tac.html     -> /docs (shell with <slot />)
  docs/_topic/
    tac.html        -> /docs/:topic

sections.heading: Backend routes
sections.body: A route file at server/routes/<path>/yon.<ext> exports a Handler class whose static methods are HTTP verbs. One route can be split across languages with sibling yon files declaring non-overlapping verbs — the Tachyon repo's full-stack fixture app splits its /language route across JavaScript (GET/HEAD), Rust (POST/DELETE) and C++ (PUT/PATCH).

sections.heading: Validation
sections.body: An OPTIONS.schema.json beside each handler validates requests and responses with CHEX regex schemas, and feeds the generated OpenAPI 3.1 document served at /openapi.json with a docs UI at /api-docs.

yon.title: Yon backend
yon.summary: Yon is Tachyon's backend/runtime layer: filesystem-routed handlers, polyglot process adapters, CHEX validation, OpenAPI docs, FYLO data routes, SSE realtime, middleware, rate limiting and OpenTelemetry.

sections.heading: Serve modes
sections.body: ty serve is shape-aware. A client/ app serves frontend assets, a server/ app serves backend routes, a client/ + server/ app runs full-stack, and client/ + db/ can mount Tachyon's built-in FYLO browser routes when YON_DATA_BROWSER_ENABLED=true.
sections.code:
ty serve
YON_PORT=8000 ty serve
YON_HOST=0.0.0.0 ty serve
NODE_ENV=production ty serve

sections.heading: Route convention
sections.body: Backend routes live at server/routes/<path>/yon.<ext>. A Handler class exposes static methods named after HTTP verbs. Dynamic path segments use _name folders and arrive in request.paths.

sections.heading: Languages
sections.body: Yon ships ergonomic adapters for JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Ruby, PHP, Dart, Java, C#, C++ and Rust. The adapter path lets authors write only Handler methods. Any other executable language can still run by speaking Yon JSON over stdin/stdout and declaring methods in OPTIONS.schema.json.
sections.code:
server/yon.providers.js     # optional app-defined providers
.tachyonrc                 # optional interpreter overrides

sections.heading: Process lifecycle
sections.body: Yon invokes route handlers as processes and prewarms handlers after route discovery. When NODE_ENV=production, compiled-language adapters cache their build artifacts so later requests avoid recompilation work.
sections.code:
YON_HANDLER_TIMEOUT_MS=30000
YON_MAX_BODY_BYTES=1048576

sections.heading: Validation and status selection
sections.body: OPTIONS.schema.json is strictly CHEX. Request schemas validate declared request sections, response schemas validate the returned body, and status-code schemas let Yon infer the final HTTP status from the body shape.

sections.heading: OpenAPI and docs UI
sections.body: Yon generates OpenAPI 3.1 from registered routes and OPTIONS.schema.json files. The JSON document is served at /openapi.json and the self-hosted Tachyon API docs UI is served at /api-docs by default.
sections.code:
curl http://localhost:8080/openapi.json
open http://localhost:8080/api-docs

sections.heading: FYLO routes
sections.body: When YON_DATA_BROWSER_ENABLED=true, Yon mounts /_fylo for collection inspection and REST-style document operations. Keep it readonly by default; enable mutation only behind auth, explicit origins and rate limiting.
sections.code:
YON_DATA_BROWSER_ENABLED=true
YON_DATA_BROWSER_READONLY=true
YON_DATA_BROWSER_READONLY=false

sections.heading: Realtime mailboxes
sections.body: YON_REALTIME_ENABLED mounts /_yon/realtime. Clients register a TTID, hold or reopen an SSE stream, and messages are stored durably in FYLO-backed topic logs for replay after reconnect.
sections.code:
YON_REALTIME_ENABLED=true
curl -X POST /_yon/realtime/clients
curl '/_yon/realtime/stream?clientId=<TTID>'

sections.heading: Security and middleware
sections.body: Yon rejects disallowed origins before route execution, applies security headers, supports Basic Auth hashes, request/body limits, middleware before/after hooks, and an optional app-provided rateLimiter.take(). Legacy middleware.js still works, and polyglot middleware can live at server/middleware/yon.<ext> or middleware/yon.<ext>.
sections.code:
YON_ALLOW_ORIGINS=https://app.example
YON_BASIC_AUTH_HASH=...
YON_RATE_LIMIT_MAX=120
YON_RATE_LIMIT_WINDOW_MS=60000

sections.heading: Polyglot middleware
sections.body: Use middleware.js when you want direct JavaScript Request and Response objects. Use polyglot middleware when the app team wants the same cross-language process model as Yon routes. Tachyon looks for server/middleware/yon.<ext> first, then middleware/yon.<ext>.
sections.code:
server/
  middleware/
    yon.py          # class-style Middleware adapter
  routes/
    items/yon.ts

# or, for package-level middleware:
middleware/
  yon.rb

sections.heading: Class-style middleware
sections.body: JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Ruby and PHP can define a Middleware class. before can return a response envelope to stop the request, after can add headers or replace the response, and rateLimit can return the same decision object as middleware.js rateLimiter.take().

sections.heading: Raw shim middleware
sections.body: Any other language can skip the class adapter and use the raw protocol. The middleware file reads a JSON envelope from stdin and writes one JSON result to stdout. This is useful for Go, Swift, Zig, shell scripts, or a prebuilt binary.

sections.heading: Middleware envelopes
sections.body: Process middleware receives metadata-only envelopes by default so it cannot accidentally consume request bodies or break streaming responses. The before envelope includes phase, request and context. The after envelope also includes response metadata. Result envelopes can continue, respond early, replace a response, or return a rate-limit decision.

sections.heading: Telemetry
sections.body: With YON_OTEL_ENABLED=true, Yon creates request and handler spans, continues traceparent, returns Traceparent and X-Trace-Id headers, and persists OTLP JSON trace documents into a FYLO collection.
sections.code:
YON_OTEL_ENABLED=true
YON_OTEL_SERVICE_NAME=my-api
YON_OTEL_COLLECTION=otel-spans

templates.title: Tac templates
templates.summary: Templates are HTML with braces for interpolation, on:event attributes for delegated events, and loop, logic, and switch elements for control flow.

sections.heading: Interpolation and events
sections.body: Braces evaluate JavaScript inside the async render function, so await is available anywhere. Event handlers are single expressions compiled to one delegated listener per event type, registered at the document and hydrated off the critical path with lossless replay.
sections.code: {headline}

sections.heading: Control flow
sections.body: Loops, conditionals and switches are elements, so structure stays visible in the markup.
sections.code:
{post.title}

Ready.

Working…

Done.

sections.heading: Components
sections.body: A component folder under client/components maps to a hyphenated tag: components/panel/users becomes panel-users. Props pass as attributes, and adding the lazy attribute defers loading until the component scrolls into view.

companions.title: Companions
companions.summary: A tac.js or tac.ts file beside a template is instantiated automatically; its fields and methods are visible to the template without wiring.

sections.heading: Reactive fields
sections.body: Assigning to a declared field schedules one batched rerender of the component subtree. Fields prefixed with $ persist to sessionStorage; $$ fields persist to localStorage. Props auto-bind onto same-named declared fields.

sections.heading: Decorators
sections.body: Stage 3 decorators expose the same helpers declaratively — publish and subscribe for cross-component signals, env for allowlisted browser config, and onMount for lifecycle. The compiler auto-imports them.

workers.title: Tac Workers
workers.summary: Browser-local worker backends shaped like Yon routes, compiled in-house to WebAssembly with no external toolchain, and invoked with fetch("tac://…").

sections.heading: Author like a route
sections.body: A worker at client/workers/language/rust/tac.rs exposes verb methods. The current Tac compiler ships Rust, JavaScript and TypeScript worker providers. Rust is the cross-target provider; JavaScript and TypeScript are web-scoped providers that can run through the browser fallback where a native host supports it.

sections.heading: Invoke with fetch
sections.body: The request verb selects the handler method, and heavy routes can opt into a worker pool. A small platform boundary exposes curated facts like os, arch and timezone through request.platform().

sections.heading: Schemas
sections.body: An OPTIONS.schema.json beside the worker validates request and response payloads in the bundled browser runtime, so frontend workers keep the same contract-first shape as Yon routes.

sections.heading: Peer boundary
sections.body: When the app opts in with TAC_NATIVE_CAPABILITIES=peer.*, Tac workers can ask the page runtime for brokered WebRTC data-channel operations. The app still owns signaling and TURN infrastructure.

devices.title: Devices and targets
devices.summary: Tac builds one client source tree into browser, desktop and mobile outputs. The selected target is embedded into the shell and exposed to templates, scripts and workers.

sections.heading: Targets
sections.body: The supported bundle targets are web, macos, windows, linux, ios and android. The all target expands to every supported target and writes sibling directories under dist/.
sections.code:
ty bundle --target web      # dist/web
ty bundle --target macos    # dist/macos
ty bundle --target windows  # dist/windows
ty bundle --target linux    # dist/linux
ty bundle --target ios      # dist/ios
ty bundle --target android  # dist/android
ty bundle --target all

sections.heading: Preview
sections.body: Preview accepts exactly one target. If you omit --target, Tachyon previews web. Native previews run prerequisite checks first so missing Xcode, Android SDK, WebView2, GTK or WebKitGTK dependencies fail early with an actionable message.
sections.code:
ty preview --target web
ty preview --target macos
ty preview --target android

sections.heading: Runtime globals
sections.body: Templates can read environment, platform, target and os without imports. Scripts can read the structured context at this.tac.platform, and workers can read the same facts with request.platform("key").
sections.code:
<switch :value="environment">
  <case :when="'desktop'">Desktop {platform}</case>
  <case :when="'mobile'">Mobile {platform}</case>
  <case default>Browser {platform}</case>
</switch>

const mode = this.tac.platform.environment

sections.heading: Environment model
sections.body: web maps to environment=browser. macos, windows and linux map to environment=desktop. ios and android map to environment=mobile. Native targets keep platform equal to the selected OS; the web target detects the visitor OS separately when the browser exposes it.

storage.title: Storage and data
storage.summary: FYLO is filesystem-first: collections, indexes, event journals and WORM history all live under one root that Yon serves through /_fylo.

sections.heading: Local-first browser reads
sections.body: Inside Tac scripts, fetch() caches successful GET responses into IndexedDB, and the compiler-injected fylo client mirrors documents into OPFS with cache-first, network-first, reload and no-store policies.

sections.heading: Live subscriptions
sections.body: subscribe() runs an initial find, listens to the browser-local mirror for same-tab mutations, and follows the server event stream for remote changes — falling back to polling when SSE is unavailable.

sections.heading: Realtime mailboxes
sections.body: Yon realtime persists messages into durable FYLO queues and delivers them over Server-Sent Events; reconnecting clients replay from their last cursor, so offline browsers never lose messages.

cookbook.title: Cookbook
cookbook.summary: Concrete recipes for building Tachyon apps without leaving the frontend-first workflow. This website is frontend-only, so these patterns can be explored with Tac workers and the browser FYLO client before a Yon server exists.

sections.heading: Frontend-only prototype
sections.body: Start with client/ and db/ only. Tac renders pages and components, workers mimic compute-heavy endpoints through tac:// URLs, and the browser FYLO client stores documents locally. When you later add server/routes, the UI contract can stay the same.
sections.code:
client/
  pages/tac.html
  components/panel/users/tac.html
  workers/language/rust/tac.rs
  shared/data/docs.json
db/
  schemas/users/manifest.json

sections.heading: Local-first collection
sections.body: Use fylo.<collection> from a Tac companion to read and write documents through the browser mirror. The wrapper keeps IndexedDB and OPFS as the local cache, then can synchronize with Yon /_fylo routes when a backend is present.

sections.heading: Signal-driven UI
sections.body: Publish and subscribe decorators replace manual event wiring between companion scripts. A form can publish a collection change and every interested panel can refresh itself without knowing who sent the signal.

sections.heading: Polyglot middleware boundary
sections.body: When the frontend prototype grows a Yon server, add middleware at server/middleware/yon.<ext>. Class-style middleware works for the supported adapter languages, and the raw JSON shim lets a team keep an existing policy engine in another executable language.

sections.heading: Native target polish
sections.body: Design once against the environment and platform globals, then bundle to web, desktop and mobile outputs. The same Tac template can choose browser, desktop or mobile copy without creating separate pages.
sections.code:
<switch :value="environment">
  <case :when="'desktop'">Desktop workspace for {platform}</case>
  <case :when="'mobile'">Pocket workspace for {platform}</case>
  <case default>Browser workspace</case>
</switch>

operations.title: Operations
operations.summary: Health and readiness endpoints, generated OpenAPI docs, middleware hooks, rate limiting, and OpenTelemetry spans persisted into FYLO come built in.

sections.heading: Observability
sections.body: With YON_OTEL_ENABLED, Yon writes request and handler spans as OTLP JSON into the otel-spans collection, continues incoming traceparent headers, and emits Traceparent and X-Trace-Id on responses. This site's telemetry panel reads them back from /language/telemetry.

sections.heading: Build and deploy
sections.body: ty bundle writes dist/web by default. Use --target all during release validation, then preview or package the individual target you intend to ship.
sections.code:
ty bundle --target all
ty preview --target web
ty preview --target macos

sections.heading: Security
sections.body: Security headers on every response, hashed Basic Auth, request limits and handler timeouts, origin-aware CORS rejection, and schema validation are all on by default in production.
